data.title: Did You Catch The Uno Reverse Card? Initiating Apple With A Buy
data.description:
Investment thesis

Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) has used China as a helping hand before in manufacturing, but now, for the first time, the company is using a top Chinese player, Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA), to boost domestic iPhone sales in what is now a struggling Chinese market for the former. I’m initiating Apple with a buy for the mid-to-long term investor in light of this. I think the new Alibaba partnership is good news for both companies. It’s a win-win, more weighted towards Apple, I must say.

In terms of Apple, I think the company could use a helping hand in its Chinese market, which has had declining sales for a few quarters now amid weak consumer spending and intensified competition. I think the stakes for Apple losing a stronghold within the Chinese market are high, to say the least, which is why I think it has all the more to gain from this partnership through staying compliant with regulations, improving service performance, and avoiding reliance on government-run providers. Also, one thing worth mentioning is Apple doesn’t feel the DeepSeek, from Hangzhou DeepSeek Artificial Intelligence Co., Ltd. (DEEPSEEK), heat like some other guys, including Google (GOOGL) (GOOG), since it has long focused on integrating AI models into its hardware as opposed to investing billions in building its own, meaning with cheaper AI models like the one DeepSeek offers, the DeepSeek moment proved positive for the company. CEO Tim Cook said in response to a question about DeepSeek:

I think innovation that drives efficiency is a good thing.

I’m optimistic about Apple regaining some of its anemic losses in the highly competitive Chinese market with this partnership due to the absence of Apple intelligence in its China iPhones and the hindrance that has on the sales in the region.

Why did Apple pick Alibaba over Baidu and DeepSeek?

Apple picked Alibaba’s Qwen model as its AI service provider for the Chinese market, and chairman Joseph Tsai confirmed the partnership during the World Government Summit on February 13th. But before taking a decision so big, the company looked into many others, but ultimately, Alibaba was the chosen one. This comes at a crucial time for Apple, whose sales and shares in the Chinese market have been bleeding over the past few quarters, as previously mentioned, with Cook attributing iPhone sales decelerating in China to the absence of Apple Intelligence. I share the sentiment, even more so with Evercore ISI noting that iPhone sales had stronger performance in regions where Apple intelligence was available. Additionally, according to recent data, over 50% of Chinese iPhone users said the delay in AI was the main reason they opted not to upgrade.

Anyway, due to strict regulations in China, Apple can't introduce its full Apple intelligence features directly into the Chinese market and needs to collaborate with local companies (including Tencent, ByteDance, and DeepSeek), with only a few companies meeting the criteria. At the same time, Apple requires robust corporate strength and partners who can meet its 1. Standards, and 2. its customization requirements. Alibaba emerged as the best choice due to the performance of its Qwen-VL model being a top-ranked open-source large language model. According to the latest Hugging Face rankings, all top ten models are derived from secondary training based on Qwen, surpassing Meta’s Llama. Currently, the number of derivative models based on Qwen is over 90,000, which further sets in stone the fact that Alibaba can meet Apple’s high standards. In 1Q24, Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct achieved a download share of 26.6%, taking it to the top as the most favored open-source model among global developers. So why not DeepSeek? If you were to compare Alibaba’s advantage as a long-standing AI Chinese giant, DeepSeek and the like are still in the process of understanding the local users' needs and are miles away from offering the support needed for a quick implementation process. As for Baidu, Apple is still working with the company to develop AI features for iPhone users in China, but as it faced setbacks, it changed course.

Apple revenue breakdown & China pain points

While I’ve been eying the stock for a while, it was only now that I found a clear catalyst ahead. Apple gets around 56% of revenue through its iPhone sales, with Mac accounting for 7%, iPads at 6.5%, wearables, home and accessories at 9.4%, and services at 21.2%. So, it shouldn’t come as a surprise to you that I focus mainly on iPhone sales. If we were to break this down by region, the company gets over 42% of revenue from the U.S., its home market, 27% from Europe, 14.8% from Greater China, 7.2% from Japan, and 8.2% from the rest of Asia Pacific. So again, it shouldn’t come as a surprise when I flag Greater China making up less and less of the revenue, now standing at 14.8% for 1Q25, down from 17.4% in 1Q24 and 20.4% in 1Q23.

To make matters worse, according to a Canalys report, iPhone shipments in China were down 25% in 4Q24, while Huawei’s were up 24% during the same period. I’m not saying Apple’s sales suffer only due to competition from government-supported Huawei, but that seems to be the main mover. Apple shipped 13.1 million iPhones in China during 4Q and maintained its market leadership with a 17% share, as seen below, matching Huawei and Vivo. Total smartphone shipments in China were up 5% year over year in 4Q to reach 77.4 million, with Huawei ranking second in shipments, up from 10.4 million in a year ago quarter to 12.9 million.

In terms of the full year 2024, China’s smartphone market started recovering after declining in the past few years, with FY24 shipments up 4% to 285 units. While Vivo led, with 17% of the market share and 49.3 million units shipped, Huawei came second with 16% of the market share at 46 million units, up 37%, while Apple, Oppo, and Honor all came in third with 15% market share, as seen below.

I thought maybe it would take a Hail Mary to help change these numbers, and maybe that’s what this partnership is. The company, as it did in May a year ago, offered discounts for the Lunar New Year of up to 500 yuan off of the iPhone 16 Pro and Pro Max and another 400 yuan off the iPhone 16 and 16 Plus, with older models also getting some discounts. The company has resisted offering discounts through its retail channels for years, but such tough competition needs a tough act. Though I’m not pricing in the positives of the partnership too soon, Apple’s developer conference in Shanghai on March 25 should give us a better picture.

Stock performance

I’ve never covered Apple on Seeking Alpha, but I’m glad I can’t say this after today as I initiate the stock with a buy.

Apple's stock has been struggling, with the stock down by over 2% due to headwinds from its China sales since the start of the year. I think investors should buy this concern as I believe the company has just partnered up with its salvation within China: Alibaba, or more accurately, AI.

The stock is also struggling in the six months, as seen above, with the stock up 8%, lower than the S&P500. On the three-month chart, there has been some recovery, shown below, a lot of which comes from the recent partnership news, but I think there is still a lot more room for upside, considering that we haven't seen these positives reflected on the numbers yet.

Valuation

Apple has a market cap of $3.67 trillion. The stock price was up over 34% in the last 52 weeks, with a beta of 0.92, according to Seeking Alpha, making Apple’s price volatility higher than the market average. Street analysts are cautiously optimistic about Apple. Around 21% of street analysts are a strong buy on Apple, 45.8% a buy, 25% a hold, 6.3% a sell, and the rest a strong sell, according to data from Refinitiv. Regarding price targets, Apple is witnessing an upward trend. The PT median was $247.5 in November, up to $250 through December and January, and is now at $254, while the PT mean was at $241.6 in November, up to $243.6 in January, and is now at $250. I think Apple’s upward trend shows a favorable investment at current levels.

On the valuation metrics, Apple has a forward Price/Book ratio of 60.4, higher than the sector median at 4.4, forward PE at 33.2, also higher than the sector median at 25.3, and forward EV/Sales at 8.8, again higher than the sector median at 3.4.

In terms of growth and profitability, the company’s revenue growth of 3% falls short of the sector median at 5%. In contrast, profitability seems to be doing better, with a net income margin of 24%, surpassing the sector median at 4%.

As for earnings revisions, analysts are seemingly extremely cautious about Apple. Apple has 21 downward EPS revisions and 12 upward. I think investors shouldn't be too concerned about this as it works to the benefit of the shareholder jumping in now, considering it lowers expectations, making them more beatable.

What’s next

No investment is without risk, and Apple is no different. Apple is looking at a tough, competitive market in China, even with the incorporation of Alibaba. I don’t think discounts will cut it. The company plans to launch a new product, according to Cook in a post on X, which caused the stock to go up around 2% on Thursday. This just might be the push needed to reverse some of that negative market sentiment.

According to a Bloomberg News report last week, a new low-end iPhone will be released this month; with Apple stores running out of inventory of the current iPhone SE, the new version is expected to resemble the iPhone 14, although it's rumored to have a larger screen and Face ID. This model is also expected to include the infamous Apple Intelligence, of course, the A18 chip, and Apple's in-house cellular modem chip. Now, this is expected to be one of several announcements the company has for the first half of the year, but I'm personally placing the most weight here because we could be looking at additional catalysts to help struggling sales.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Brookfield Infrastructure: Data-Driven Growth With An Attractive Yield
data.description:
Introduction

Brookfield Infrastructure (NYSE:BIP) (NYSE:BIPC) stands to benefit from the coming growth of the data and digital infrastructure required across the world. The diversified partnership has significant investments across the transportation, midstream, utility, and data sectors ranging from Brazilian toll roads and gas pipelines to cutting-edge data centres offering a unique blend of traditional and digital infrastructure assets.

Despite currently only representing 11% of its portfolio, the data sector is set to grow rapidly with this sector representing over 70% of the capital backlog driven by demands from cloud computing and AI. This strategic positioning, combined with Brookfield Infrastructure’s record of strong FFO growth, and an attractive dividend yield make it an attractive investment. Over the past year, the shares have risen 7%.

In this article, I will explore how Brookfield Infrastructure is leveraging the growing demand for data infrastructure to drive growth, analyze its latest financial results, and explain why I believe Brookfield Infrastructure is well positioned to reward shareholders in a world where demand for data infrastructure is soaring, and traditional infrastructure is still needed.

Why Brookfield Infrastructure?

Brookfield Infrastructure invests in a diverse range of infrastructure assets across four main segments: Utilities, Midstream, Transportation, and Data. These investments are managed by the investment firm Brookfield Asset Management (BAM) and are geographically diversified with 68% of FFO from the Americas, 17% from Europe, and 15% from the Asia Pacific region.

The firms’ assets range from toll roads, and gas pipelines, to data centres and electricity grids. Given the nature of its operations, many are in regulated sectors which, although in some cases limits upside, means 85% of its funds from operations are protected from or indexed to inflation.

Brookfield Infrastructure has a strong track record of growth in FFO per unit, averaging 15% CAGR over the past 8 years. Management has a growth target of 10% FFO growth per year. Due to this strong continuous growth in FFO, Brookfield Infrastructure has a track record of growing distributions to shareholders, averaging 9% CAGR growth per year, as it aims to return between 60-70% of FFO to investors. Given its strong growth in FFO over the years, sustainable payout ratio, and future investment pipeline, there is reason to believe Brookfield Infrastructure can continue to grow its distributions to shareholders each year.

Traditionally, the transportation segment has been the largest and most important, but in recent years the focus has switched to data assets driven by demand for cloud computing and AI. In the latest quarter, the data segment saw FFO growth of 52% from the previous year. This was in part driven by the data segment now representing $1.187 billion of growth capital expenditure, 54% of all growth CapEx despite this segment currently only accounting for 11% of FFO.

This growth in the data segment looks set to continue into the future, with the data segment now representing over 70% of the capital backlog. This is, in part, driven by the exponential increase in data consumption across the globe, whether from AI to data centres to semiconductor demand, driving the need for infrastructure to meet this.

With these significant investments in the data segment making it the fastest growing segment, the CEO of infrastructure highlighted in the Q4 2024 earnings call that he anticipates this to become Brookfield Infrastructure's largest segment within five years:

We expect growth in this sector to persist outpacing all other areas of our business and positioning it to become our largest sector within 5 years

Some of the investments in the data segment include a $30 billion semiconductor manufacturing facility in Arizona, a current 905 MW of data centre capacity which has the potential to grow to upwards of 2.8 GW capacity, and the recent acquisition of 76,000 telecom towers in India. With much of this capital deployment still not operational or in early stages, the full-value and potential growth to FFO is not currently reflected in the financial results.

Overall, Brookfield Infrastructure’s investments in high-growth digital infrastructure, combined with its more stable, infrastructure assets which are 85% protected from inflation, position it well for continued FFO growth. With a strong track record of growing shareholder returns and a focus on emerging data opportunities, Brookfield Infrastructure is positioned for long-term value creation.

Latest Results

Brookfield Infrastructure recently reported its results for Q4 2024 which displayed another quarter of growth. Funds from Operations, FFO, was reported at $646 million or $0.82 per unit, a 4% rise from the previous year. This growth was primarily due to organic expansion, including a 7% increase from annual rate adjustments, volume growth across critical infrastructure networks, and higher utilization rates in midstream operations. Brookfield Infrastructure continued its history of distribution growth with the quarterly dividend coming in at $0.405 per unit, a 6% increase from the previous year, putting the payout ratio at 64%, well, within the 60-70% long-term target. Overall net income rose to $265 million, $0.22 per unit, compared to the $73 million loss observed the previous year. This improvement was largely due to revaluation of the data centre properties the firm owns, and currency hedging.

Looking at a segment level breakdown, we see a mixed picture. The utilities segment saw FFO, net income, and EBITDA fall, as, despite benefitting from inflation indexation and growth in customers in the UK business, depreciation of the Brazilian Real and increased borrowing costs in Brazil negatively impacted results.

Both the transport and midstream segments saw results relatively stable compared to the previous year. Transport operations delivered $295 million in FFO, a slight increase from $292 million in the previous year. The segment was helped from inflationary tariff increases and higher utilization rates at Triton, Brookfield Infrastructure’s global intermodal logistics operation. However, results were partially offset by the depreciation of the Brazilian real and increased borrowing costs. The midstream segment posted $165 million in FFO, compared to $162 million in Q4 2023, as contracted and market sensitive revenues improved. However, higher borrowing costs linked to upfinancings at Brookfield Infrastructure’s US gas pipeline and North American gas storage operations constrained growth.

Moving to the data segment, we see Brookfield Infrastructure’s focus on data investments start to payoff. FFO for this segment came in at $102 million, up from $67 million a year earlier, with big increases also seen in EBITDA and net income, driven by heightened demand for digital infrastructure. The segment benefited from the inclusion of 76,000 telecom tower sites that were acquired in India, along with the commissioning of 255 megawatts within its global data center portfolio.

Overall, Brookfield Infrastructure had a solid Q4 2024, with FFO continuing its upward trend. The ongoing growth in demand for digital infrastructure is continued to be taken advantage of, with significant growth from last year, helping support management’s aim to grow FFO 10% annually. With a large backlog of investments in this high-growth segment, Brookfield Infrastructure is well-positioned going forward.

Valuation

With an FFO of $3.12 in 2024, up from $2.95 in 2024, Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) is now trading on a price to FFO multiple of 10.5, a rather undemanding valuation given it has traded significantly higher in the past. On a free cash flow basis, the partnership again is trading at a discount to the historic average at a price to free cash flow multiple of only 3.26 against a five-year average of 5.26, despite there having been consistent cash flow growth over the period.

With FFO growth set to persist, driven in part by expanding investments in the data segments, these multiples appear low. Along with management's track record of generating solid growth and targeting 10% yearly FFO growth, I believe Brookfield Infrastructure is an appealing opportunity, especially with its attractive dividend yield.

There are two main ways to gain exposure to Brookfield Infrastructure. Either through Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP), which is structured as a Limited Partnership and sends out a Schedule K-1, or through the economically identical Brookfield Infrastructure Corporation (BIPC), which is structured as a Canadian Corporation and sends out a Form 1099. While economically identical, BIP currently trades at an $8.69 discount to BIPC, offering a more favorable entry point for investors willing to deal with the K-1 form.

Risks

When investing in Brookfield Infrastructure, there are several risks one must consider.

Firstly, the management fees the partnership must pay to its asset manager, Brookfield (BAM). Brookfield Infrastructure benefits from Brookfield Asset Management’s ownership of 30%, helping align interests, and reward performance and value creation; however, this structure does not come cheap, with a management fee of 1.25% of the combined market value of the partnership and BIPC plus preferred units outstanding and net recourse debt. In total, this costs BIP around $400 million a year, having an impact on distributional cash flow.

Whilst many of Brookfield Infrastructure’s assets, which include regulated utilities and other infrastructure, benefit from revenues which are inflation linked protecting against rising costs, higher interest rates caused by higher inflation present a risk. Although a large portion of its debt is at fixed interest rates, rising interest rates lower the present value of existing assets, impacting valuations and project & acquisition economics. Also, a longer-term rise in interest rates cannot be fully protected against as debt needs to be refinanced as the terms expire, leading to higher interest expenses putting pressure on cash flows and potentially impacting distributions.

Finally, given Brookfield Infrastructure’s exposure to both North American mid-stream assets, and transportation assets, it risks being harmed by potential tariffs between the US and Canada. Whilst these tariffs have been postponed for now this adds uncertainty to operations, and if they do go ahead will likely decrease cross-border energy flows and transportation volumes, impacting these assets profitability.

Conclusion

Brookfield Infrastructure stands to benefit from the increasing need for digital infrastructure, with assets in its data segment accounting for over 70% of its capital backlog. Driven by surging demand from cloud computing and AI, Brookfield Infrastructure stands to benefit from this surge in demand. With its diverse portfolio of other infrastructure assets, healthy FFO growth, and an attractive dividend yield, Brookfield Infrastructure has solid fundamentals. Trading at a discount to previous valuations, it presents an attractive entry point for investors seeking both income and growth. I believe Brookfield Infrastructure shares are a buy.

data.title: Micron: Improved Certainty And Low Valuation Support Bullish Thesis
data.description:
Micron Technology's (NASDAQ:MU) stock has been moving sideways in the last seven months, seemingly dropping on every piece of news but then recovering slowly. Since the general long-term thesis remains bullish for Micron, this period of prolonged consolidation seems like a good time to accumulate a bigger position in the stock. Continuing AI spend from big players and Micron's low valuation are still strong enough reasons to buy the stock. And with the recent updates, which cleared uncertainty regarding MU's short- to medium-term future, the next growth cycle might come soon.

The company's updates for FQ3 reduce short-term uncertainty

During the recent Wolfe conference, Micron provided important updates on its potential FQ3 performance and cleared any uncertainty that could have remained for the upcoming FQ2. On the latter, the company expects no change or update, which means Micron is sticking to its previous guidance. As a reminder, the corporation expects around $7.9 billion in revenue and $1.43 in non-GAAP EPS. This would lead to a 36% and a 240% increase year-over-year, respectively.

For FQ3, important updates include margin development, revenue mix, and competition.

Starting with margins, the general headline might sound negative from the first glance — the company expects its FQ3 gross margin "a few" percentage points lower sequentially. Since Micron guided for about 37.5-39.5% gross margin in FQ2, FQ3 gross margin should then be around 35%.

However, reading Micron management's entire message, it turns out this lower gross margin is primarily driven by stronger-than-expected consumer-oriented growth in the quarter, namely for PC and smartphone products. These product lines are generally associated with lower margins, compared to other segments, like data center. Therefore, with more revenue coming from consumer-related products in FQ3, MU's total gross margin will be "diluted" to a lower number.

Since the company confirmed general strength and continued growth in other segments, the commentary about consumer-related strength means MU's total revenue might be significantly higher in FQ3 than originally expected. Therefore, even with this lower gross margin, Micron's earnings are likely to get a boost in FQ3 thanks to the higher total revenue base. The company did also mention that the underutilization in NAND impacts margins as well, but from what I understand, this impact is not significant. NAND is also expected to return to normal over time thanks to supply reductions.

Another positive signal regarding the revenue mix is Micron's commentary on client and smartphone inventory levels. Mark Murphy, Micron's CFO, mentioned that the company now expects the inventory there to clear "by spring," with a significant content uplift of about 50% for "AI-related" devices. These likely include smartphones with intelligence features like Google's (GOOG) Pixel phones, Samsung's (OTCPK:SSNLF) Galaxy devices, and PCs with Microsoft's (MSFT) Copilot. On the latter, Micron mentioned the Windows update to be a significant driver for sales at the end of 2025, as Microsoft ends support for Windows 10 in October this year. These developments in the consumer market are a big positive for MU, as the stock's post-FQ1 drop was primarily driven by the weakness in this segment.

Additionally, what I found interesting from this conference was Micron's commentary regarding its HBM efforts and Samsung's potential success in this market.

Micron's HBM memory has been sold out for 2025 since last year, and the company expects this trend to continue as they ramp up the production of the 12-stack HBM, which is a new product claimed to be "20% less power at 50% more capacity." HBM4 also appears on track for 2026.

Regarding Samsung, it is reassuring that Micron's "base-case" scenario is that Samsung will succeed in properly entering the market. Currently, Samsung trails behind SK Hynix and Micron in HBM, despite the fact that the former has so far been very successful in other memory segments. Still, with this potential new rival in HBM, Mark Murphy claims their technology and position in the market should allow Micron to retain significant market share there.

Clearly, most of the updates the company gave during the conference are just commentary at this point, and we need to see how it will all play out in reality. However, in general, I see a lot of short- to medium-term uncertainty being reduced after these updates, which should help the stock continue its uptrend from the current consolidation.

Micron will continue to profit from AI infrastructure spend, thus breaking the usual cyclicality

When it comes to Micron's long-term potential, the situation remains favorable, in my opinion. In the latest quarter, Micron generated 55% of its revenue from the data center and networking segment, up from 20% a year ago. Since the AI and data infrastructure investments are expected to continue expanding in the near future, Micron's sales should keep growing as well, which is likely to break the usual memory cyclicality patterns.

Micron's HBM products should be a significant revenue driver here. The company mentioned they expect HBM's TAM to reach more than $100 billion by 2030. With about 20-25% market share targeted by the company, this segment alone could bring $20-25 billion annually in just 4 years.

We did put out some markers on, for example, the size of HBM and going from $16 billion TAM last year to over $100 billion by 2030. So those are big new markets. And then, you’ve got a number of associated other product lines that will grow sort of in that manner as well.

In the consumer market, Micron is likely to benefit from the fact that the share of AI-powered smartphones is expected to increase from 16% in 2024 to 54% in 2028. These devices require increased amounts of memory to operate, thus increasing potential content for MU, which explains the revenue mix update we discussed above. Again, these developments should clear the post-FQ1 negativity, as the drop in the stock price was primarily driven by the consumer segment's weakness.

Micron's valuation remains low in light of strong growth and higher certainty

Despite the improved certainty in FY2025 and its promising future beyond that, Micron continues to be valued surprisingly low, even for this cyclical company. MU's EV/EBITDA is consolidating around 6-7, way lower than FY2024's average of 12-14.

The company is also valued substantially lower compared to most other semiconductor peers. MU's PEG ratio, which shows P/E relative to earnings growth, is dismal 0.38, way lower than the "normal" level of 1.

Sure, Micron is a cyclical company and will likely be valued lower than some of its peers with a more stable growth, like Nvidia (NVDA), Texas Instruments (TXN), or AMD (AMD). However, Micron is currently only at the beginning of its unprecedented upcycle, which might remain for longer than usual thanks to the continuing AI infrastructure spend by the big players. In light of that, the current valuation seems unusually low.

Additionally, Seeking Alpha Quant gives MU a Hold rating. The stock has exceptionally strong ratings for key aspects like valuation, growth, and profitability. If MU gains some momentum after the current period of consolidation, the rating should quickly change to Buy or Strong Buy.

DCF update

To further understand the intrinsic value of MU, we can use a DCF analysis. In light of the latest updates and data, I have adjusted the assumptions slightly compared to my previous article, but the model remains the same. A quick note on the changes:

  • The expected revenue growth for FY2025 was lowered to 39% to better reflect Micron's own estimates. Revenue growth assumption for FY2026 was increased to 28%, based on analysts' estimates.
  • The risk-free return rate used in WACC to discount cash flows was increased to 4%, as the US 10-year treasury yield has grown over the last months due to lower number of rate cuts expected in 2025. From here, the WACC is estimated to be 12.9%, with a 1.18 beta and 9% market premium I use in my analysis.
  • Balance sheet numbers were adjusted based on the latest report.

Here is the operating and balance sheet data and assumptions used in the model:

From here, the current fair price range for MU is about $111-124, about 17% higher than the current level. The price target for the end of FY2025 (Micron's financial year ends in August) is about $125.

The margin of safety is not the highest I've seen for Micron, but still substantial. The model also uses relatively conservative assumptions for revenue growth and the risk-free rate. Taking a more bullish approach with higher-end revenue estimates and a risk-free rate of 3%, the fair stock price can be around $134-150.

This price range would be in-line with the recent price target of $150 set by Citi, who also reiterated Buy rating after Micron's aforementioned Q3 update. Generally, MU's Wall Street average price target is $130 at the moment, representing a 31% upside.

Risks and key takeaways

As usual, a significant margin of safety does not necessarily mean the stock will reach its fair value any time soon. In the case of Micron, the company's future depends on continued trends in the data center and consumer markets.

Even though the tech giants have expressed their plans to spend heavily on AI infrastructure, changes in the economy like higher inflation or significant breakthroughs similar to the recent DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) situation could lead to quick changes in these plans. Moreover, as a production-heavy company, Micron still needs to invest a lot of resources in new facilities and processes, like the ramp up of the HBM4 and 4E technologies, expected around 2026. These investments are risky and might require a long time to be paid off, and Micron already has a substantial amount of long-term debt on its balance sheet, around $13.2 billion. Additionally, potential success of competitors like Samsung in high-margin markets like HBM memory might still lead to detrimental effects on Micron, even if the management expresses their confidence at the moment.

However, despite all these risks, the current consolidation in the stock price seems like another great opportunity to build a position in MU. Micron should profit significantly in the long run from the continued trends, especially when it comes to the AI technology, and the recent updates regarding FQ2 and FQ3 resolve uncertainty in the short- to medium-term. Therefore, with a solid margin of safety of about 25-30%, the stock remains a Strong Buy.

data.title: Shopify: Increased Commerce/Fintech Penetration In Booming Market - Upgrade To Buy
data.description:
SHOP's Growing Penetration/ Adoption & Robust Long-Term Growth Prospects Deserve An Upgraded Rating To Buy

We previously covered Shopify Inc. (NYSE:SHOP) (TSX:SHOP:CA) in November 2024, discussing its robust growth in FQ3'24 across numerous performance metrics, including the increased GMV and higher Annual Recurring Revenue.

Even so, we had believed that the stock's premium valuations and historical price trend implied a further downside from current levels, resulting in our Hold rating then, with investors well advised to monitor for our Buy zone to materialize.

SHOP 1Y Stock Price

Since then, SHOP has already charted new peaks as it rallies by +20%, well outperforming the wider market at +2.4% and its commerce/ e-commerce SaaS peers in varying degrees.

Much of the outperformance is naturally attributed to the (still) resilient labor market and the cooling inflation on a YoY basis, which have contributed to the consumers' robust spending trends as similarly reiterated by numerous e-commerce/ retail/ travel players, including Amazon (AMZN), Costco (COST), and Booking (BKNG).

These promising developments have already triggered the outperformance of most consumer discretionary stocks/ ETFs, SHOP included.

The same excellent numbers have also been observed in SHOP's double beat FQ4'24 performance, with revenues of $2.81B (+30% QoQ/ +31.3% YoY), richer operating margins of 16.3% (+3.4 points QoQ/ +3.3 YoY/ +13.3 from FY2019 levels of 3%), and adj EPS of $0.44 (+69.2% QoQ/ +83.3% YoY).

Most importantly, the commerce SaaS company has continued to report a growing Gross Merchandise Volume [GMV] of $94.46B (+35.5% QoQ/ +25.7% YoY) and Gross Payment Volume [GPV] of $60.7B (+41.1% QoQ/ +34.5% YoY), with it underscoring the growing success of its merchant and fintech partners.

Combined with the increasingly higher penetration for Shopify Payments at 64% (+2 points QoQ/ +4 YoY), it is apparent that SHOP's unified solutions across the commerce (offline and online)/ payment ecosystem has continued to drive its growth flywheel and cross-selling opportunities, with it directly triggering the improved operational scale as observed in the richer profit margins.

This is on top of the management's intensified AI investments, allowing the company to deliver "lower cost of ownership... faster point of sale" while helping new merchants/ larger merchants to scale faster in order to drive "operational and revenue efficiencies."

These efforts have directly contributed to SHOP's excellent FY2024 performance with Rule of 50 outperformance at 53x, based on the +31% revenue growth and 22% in free cash flow margins, compared to FY2023 levels of 39x, +26%, and 13%, respectively.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons are likely why SHOP has felt confident to offer a promising FQ1'25 guidance, with revenues growth "at a mid-twenties percentage rate on a year-over-year basis" to approximately $2.31B (+25% YoY) and richer estimated operating incomes of $190M (+120% YoY).

These numbers have naturally led to the consensus raised forward estimates, with the commerce SaaS company expected to generate accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +22.6%/ +25% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +17.8%/ +14.2%, while building upon the 5Y historical growth at +41.3%/ +111.2%, respectively, with it underscoring SHOP's bright long-term prospects as it works to build "a durable 100-year company."

SHOP's Robust Long-Term Growth Prospects

This is thanks to the projected e-commerce SaaS market size growth from $9.4B to $29.82B by 2032 at a CAGR of +15.5% and the payment SaaS market size growth from $18.4B in 2024 to $43B by 2030 at a CAGR of +15.2%, with these exemplifying why SHOP's well diversified headless commerce solutions have been highly strategic.

Combined with the still low penetration in the core geographies at 2%/ globally at 1% and the management's focus to expanding its TAM over the past few years, we believe that the commerce SaaS company remains well positioned to generate profitable growth cadence moving forward.

SHOP Valuations

These reasons may also be why the market has upgraded SHOP's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to new heights of 85.26x, up from the 1Y mean of 69.21x and the sector median of 25.53x.

The same premium valuations may also be observed in SHOP's increasingly higher FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.39x, up from the prior article levels of 1.85x, the sector median of 1.84x, and its e-commerce/ e-commerce SaaS peers, including:

  • Amazon (AMZN) at 1.67x,
  • MercadoLibre (MELI) at 2.08x,
  • Wix.com Ltd. (WIX) at 1.32x, and
  • Squarespace, Inc. (SQSP) at 1.19x,

with it underscoring why the former is increasingly expensive at current levels.

This development implies that a moderate pullback may be necessary for an improved return profile, one that we shall further discuss in the next segment.

So, Is SHOP Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

SHOP 5Y Stock Price

For now, SHOP has already run away and charted new peaks of $120s ranges, hitting the upper ranges of the three established uptrend lines since the end of 2022 and running away from its 50/ 100/ 200 days moving averages.

For context, we had offered a fair value estimate of $69.50 in our last article, based on the LTM EPS of $1.07 ending FQ3'24 (+1,237.5% sequentially) and the prior 1Y P/E mean of 65x.

Based on the FY2024 adj EPS of $1.26 (+72.6% YoY) and the new 1Y mean of 69.21x, it is apparent that SHOP has run away (yet again) from our updated fair value estimates of $87.20.

Based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $2.45, it goes without saying that the stock has already pulled forward part of its upside potential to our long-term price target of $169.50, with it underscoring the overly fast and furious rally.

Does this mean that we are reiterating our Hold rating?

Not quite.

SHOP has already traded as expected within the three established uptrend lines since the end of 2022, with the bulls easily defending the January 2025 bottom and the prior 50 days moving averages of $100s ranges.

This reason is also why we believe that the stock is likely to continue its upward momentum within these established trend lines, with it offering interested investors with the clear entry points moving forward.

Combined with the moderating short interest volume by -48.7% on a YoY basis and by -71.4% over the past two years, we are cautiously upgrading the SHOP stock to a Buy.

This is with the caveat that interested investors observe the stock price movement for a little longer before adding, preferably upon a moderate retracement to its current 50 days moving averages of $110s ranges for an improved margin of safety and expanded upside potential of +54% to our long-term price target.

For now, patience may be more prudent in the meantime.

data.title: BNY Mellon Dynamic Value Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Artificial intelligence (AI) once again drove growth stocks to handily outperform their value peers while large-cap stocks bested their small-cap counterparts.

Market Review

Equity markets were mixed in the final quarter of 2024 as the re-election of Donald J. Trump and rate cuts buoyed US equities, while tariff threats and slowing economic growth pressured other markets. For the quarter, the S&P 500® Index added 2.41%, while the MSCI EAFE Index, a measure of non-US developed markets, slid 8.11% and the MSCI Emerging Markets Index lost 8.01%. Artificial intelligence (AI) once again drove growth stocks to handily outperform their value peers while large-cap stocks bested their small-cap counterparts.

Quarterly Performance

BNY Mellon Dynamic Value Fund (Class A at NAV) returned -1.48% during the fourth quarter of 2024. In comparison, the fund’s benchmark, the Russell 1000 Value Index (the “Index”), returned -1.98% for the same time period.

Share price and investment return fluctuate, and an investor's shares may be worth more or less than original cost upon redemption. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. Performance for periods less than 1 year is not annualized. Go to BNY Mellon Investment Management - Global Home - English for the fund's most recent month-end returns. Returns assume the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, if any.

Total Expenses (12/31/24)

Gross 1

1 Gross expenses is the total annual operating expense ratio for the fund, before any fee waivers or expense reimbursements. 2 Net Expenses is the total annual operating expense ratio for the fund, after any applicable fee waivers or expense reimbursements. The net expense ratio(s) reflect a contractual expense reduction agreement through 12/31/2025, without which, the returns would have been lower. The Net Expenses is the actual fund expense ratio applicable to investors. Not all classes of shares may be available to all investors or through all broker-dealer platforms.

In the US, market advances were fueled by rate cuts in November and December, as well as hopes of pro-business policies and promises of tax cuts by President-elect Trump. However, gains were tempered in December after the US Federal Reserve signaled the potential for fewer rate cuts in 2025. Market breadth again narrowed during the quarter with over half of the sectors in the S&P 500 posting losses. In addition to AI-fueled gains in the communication services, information technology and consumer discretionary sectors, financial stocks advanced on the hopes of a more regulatory-friendly environment under the new presidential administration.

Conversely, the materials sector continued to be hampered by slowing growth in China, while health-care stocks faced multiple headwinds following the election. Economic growth remained solid, buoyed by resilient consumer spending. The 10-year US Treasury yield ended the quarter higher, and the US dollar surged on election results and President-elect Trump’s pro-US economic agenda.

Developed international markets fell as European stocks plummeted around interest-rate uncertainty and fears of tariffs under the new US presidential administration. With inflation moving closer to target, the European Central Bank reduced interest rates twice. However, economic growth remained sluggish, political strife continued in France and the German government collapsed. In Germany, the region’s largest economy, both consumer and business sentiment suffered and the risk of recession rose over the quarter. In the UK, the Bank of England cut rates in November but then held them steady in December given growing inflation forecasts. Core consumer inflation in Japan remained above the central bank's 2% target; however, the Bank of Japan left rates unchanged.

Emerging-market equities retreated for the quarter, facing pressure from a sluggish Chinese economy, a strengthening US dollar and concerns over potential tariffs. While Chinese equities rallied in December, they ended the quarter lower on concerns over economic growth. Authorities have stepped up measures to support the economy, including rate cuts, relaxed home-buying restrictions, purchases of sovereign bonds and multi-billion injections into outright reverse repurchase agreements. India’s economy slowed in the third quarter due to weaker expansion in manufacturing and decreasing consumption. In Brazil, stocks underperformed amid worries over the government's commitment to cut public spending and reign in the growing budget deficit.

Commodities advanced for the quarter, bolstered by natural-gas prices. An early and frigid start of winter in North America and Europe, in addition to the end of Russian supplies to Europe, pushed prices higher. Weak demand from China and surplus concerns continued to pressure crude oil. While starting the quarter higher, gold prices lagged due to the strengthening US dollar, the US election results and the dissipation of tensions in the Middle East. The Dow Jones Commodity Index ended the quarter up by 0.49%.

Top 10 Holdings (12/31/24)

%

4.57

3.48

3.22

2.59

The holdings listed should not be considered recommendations to buy or sell a security. Large concentrations can increase share price volatility.

Performance Review

The fund (Class A shares at NAV) outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 1000 Value Index, during the fourth quarter of 2024. Stock selection in the industrials and consumer discretionary sectors contributed, while positioning in the health care and materials sectors detracted.

Sector Review1

Positive Impacts

Industrials: Stock selection in the industrials sector contributed to returns for the quarter, helped by an overweight position to GE Vernova (GEV). Security selection, mainly not owning a number of underperforming building products names also benefited relative performance.

Consumer Discretionary: Stock selection within the automobiles subsector drove returns in the consumer discretionary sector.

Negative Impacts

Health Care: The health care sector lagged the broader markets during the fourth quarter, pressured by President-elect Trump’s choice to head the Department of Health and Human Services, Robert F. Kennedy, Jr., who was a vocal opponent of the pharmaceutical industry during the campaign. Several of the strategy’s top detractors, including Danaher Corporation and Baxter International (BAX), were in the health care sector.

Materials: An overweight to the materials sector detracted. The metals and mining subsector detracted amid slowing industrial activity in China, and included the strategy’s position in Freeport-McMoRan (FCX).

Stock Review1

Positive Impacts

EQT Corporation: Shares of the natural gas producer rose following solid third-quarter results and a slight increase in the production guide for the fourth quarter. EQT also announced the divestiture of a non-operated asset. Shares rallied over the final month of the year on forecasts for colder weather across the US.

GE Vernova: GE Vernova saw strength during the quarter following an update to its guidance, with additional insight surrounding long-term demand strength, particularly within its power and electrification segments. Demand for products in these segments remains solid as the company aims to increase production capacity to meet heightened demand for grid equipment.

Goldman Sachs Group: Shares of the investment bank gained on continued strong capital markets activity plus expectations of reduced regulatory burden under the new presidential administration.

Negative Impacts

Newmont Corporation (NEM): Shares fell after the gold mining company lowered full-year guidance and took down their mid-term targets driven by higher-than-expected costs and lower production. Additionally, the firm faced headwinds as gold prices fell following the US election results.

Danaher Corporation: Despite a third-quarter earnings beat announced in October, the company continues to see weakness stemming from its decision to maintain rather than raise its full-year revenue guidance.

Baxter International: Shares of the medical device maker were weak over the period, largely on the US election results and concerns that new tariffs would impact some of their cross-border business. The company currently operates three manufacturing plants in Mexico.

Market Outlook

At the beginning of 2024, we recommended investors take a “balanced” approach to the financial markets and value investing. This was largely predicated on a number of macro risks that were prevalent in the market, most notably the uncertainty around the Fed’s ability to achieve an economic soft landing, the outcome of the US elections and the increasing concentration of growth and technology stocks in both equity indices and investor’s portfolios. Given the wide dispersion of potential outcomes and the potential volatility around each, we thought this “balanced” approach was prudent to minimize as much of the macro risk as possible while focusing on the idiosyncratic opportunities being captured by our investment process.

While there has been some clarity on the above-mentioned issues, there remains a high degree of uncertainty and skepticism around them as well. Looking ahead to 2025, navigating these “known unknowns” will require investors to be nimble in order to take advantage of opportunities as they arise while avoiding sudden pitfalls from unexpected outcomes. We believe experienced active managers with long-tenured teams that employ a consistent and repeatable process are best suited to strike the right balance in this type of environment. We believe investing in companies that sit at the nexus of strong and improving fundamentals, attractive valuation, and catalyst-driven business momentum have the ability to deliver the best investment outcomes for our clients.

Footnote

1 Performance of individual stock evaluated is based on total effect. Total effect represents the opportunity cost of an investment manager's investment decisions relative to an overall benchmark, which can include holdings that are not held by the portfolio but contribute to relative portfolio performance.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a mutual fund carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus, or a summary prospectus, if available, that contains this and other information about a fund, contact your financial professional. For more information, call 1- 800-373-9387 or visit BNY Mellon Investment Management - Global Home - English. Read the prospectus carefully before investing. Investors should discuss with their financial professional the eligibility requirements for Class I, which are available only to certain eligible investors, and the historical results achieved by the fund’s respective share classes.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Risks

Equities are subject to market, market sector, market liquidity, issuer, and investment style risks, to varying degrees. Investing in foreign denominated and/or domiciled securities involves special risks, including changes in currency exchange rates, political, economic, and social instability, limited company information, differing auditing and legal standards, and less market liquidity. These risks generally are greater with emerging market countries.

As of 12/31/24 the companies mentioned represented 12.80% of the fund’s portfolio in the aggregate. The holdings listed should not be considered recommendations to buy or sell a particular security. Other holdings may not have performed as well as some of those listed herein. Portfolio composition is subject to change at any time.

Artificial intelligence refers to computer systems that can perform tasks typically requiring human intelligence, such as visual perception, speech recognition, decision making and language translation. Non-operating assets are assets that are not essential to a business's day- to-day operations, but can still generate income or provide a return on investment (ROI). A reverse repurchase agreement(RRP) is a short-term agreement where a seller sells securities to a buyer, with the agreement to buy them back at a higher price on a specified date. Revenue guidance, also known as earnings guidance or forward-looking statements, is a financial forecast that a company provides to investors about its expected revenue.

The Russell 1000® Value Index is an unmanaged index which measures the performance of those Russell 1000® companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth values. The S&P 500® Index is widely regarded as the best single gauge of large-cap U.S. equities. The index includes 500 leading companies and captures approximately 80% coverage of available market capitalization. This is not a benchmark for the fund. The Morgan Stanley Capital International Europe, Australasia, Far East (MSCI EAFE®) Index is a free float-adjusted, market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure equity performance in developed markets, excluding the United States and Canada. The index consists of select designated MSCI national developed market indices. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index is a free float-adjusted, market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the equity performance in global emerging markets.

The index consists of 22 MSCI emerging-market national indices. This is not a benchmark for the fund. The Dow Jones Commodity Index is a broad measure of the commodity futures market that emphasizes diversification and liquidity through a simple, straightforward, equal- weighted approach. The index tracks 28 different commodities, from agricultural to precious metals to energy products. An investor cannot invest directly in any index.

The fund's investment adviser is BNY Mellon Investment Adviser, Inc. (BNYIA). data.title: EVS Broadcast Equipment SA 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by EVS Broadcast Equipment SA in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: Foreign Stocks Lead Major Asset Classes In 2025
data.description:
After two straight years of performance dominance by US equities, foreign stocks in developed markets are leading the major asset classes by a wide margin so far in 2025, based on a set of ETFs through Tuesday’s close (Feb. 18). It’s anyone’s guess if the leadership for equities ex-US continues, but at the moment the change in fortunes is a striking reshuffling of recent history.

Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets Index Fund ETF (VEA) has surged 8.5% year to date, well ahead of the second-best performer, commodities (GCC), which is up 6.2%. In third place: emerging markets (VWO) with a 4.7% increase. US stocks (VTI) are currently the fourth-best performer via a 4.4% advance.

For context, the Global Market Index (GMI) is up 4.6% so far this year. GMI is an unmanaged benchmark (maintained by CapitalSpectator.com) that holds all the major asset classes (except cash) in market value weights via ETFs and represents a competitive benchmark for multi asset-class portfolios.

The changing fortunes mark a switch following two consecutive calendar years of strong leadership for US equities. In 2024, for example, American shares (VTI) rallied nearly 24%, far ahead of the rest of the field.

What’s driving the changing fortunes? Analyst Ed Yardeni this week suggests the Trump factor may be a reason:

“So far, Trump’s tariff turmoil seems to be weighing more on the US, Canada, Mexico, and many other emerging markets than on China and most European countries. That could change once reciprocal tariffs are actually imposed by the US in early April, unless Trump changes his mind, again.”

Whatever the catalyst, foreign developed markets are on a tear. VEA’s regional weighting tells the story. The Europe-developed category has the biggest weight in the fund: more than 40%, followed by Japan with a 21% share and the UK at 12%, according to Morningstar.com.

Meanwhile, the long-term expected return for US equities has fallen recently relative to the red-hot run over the past decade, based on modeling by CapitalSpectator.com. By contrast, ex ante performance for foreign-developed stocks has been revised higher lately.

Despite the revisions, US and foreign-developed stocks are currently expected to generate similar results in the 8%-9% annualized total return range over the long haul. Using this year’s rally in foreign shares as a guide, however, suggests that the crowd is pricing in a wider divergence in the near term.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: TSLL Can Help Traders Juice Their Tesla Trading Performance, But Beware The Risks
data.description:
Direxion Daily TSLA Bull 2X Shares ETF (NASDAQ:TSLL) is a leveraged, single-name stock ETF that aims to achieve 2x the return of the underlying Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) shares daily. This ETF was designed to provide investors with additional risk beyond directly holding TSLA shares, as well as the associated returns. Given my bullish outlook on TSLA stock, I believe actively trading TSLL can potentially add some additional positive risk to one’s trading strategy. I am rating TSLL with a BUY.

As a disclaimer, I have previously covered TSLA shares on Seeking Alpha. As a potential investor in TSLL, I highly recommend that you fully understand the various catalysts of the underlying stock before considering trading TSLL.

It should also be noted that I am bullish on TSLA shares. My rationale behind my position in Tesla is that the firm is developing a closed-loop ecosystem for autonomous robotics. Between Tesla’s Robotaxi program beginning testing in Austin, Texas, in June 2025, and California being targeted for some time in 2025, the firm is moving closer to undertaking a larger, subscription-based vehicle model. In addition to this, Tesla may be bringing its Optimus humanoid robot to the market as early as e2h26, taking on the industrials sector. Between these two initiatives, Starlink for broadband connectivity, its storage and solar panel business, and Cortex, Tesla’s AI factory, Tesla will have complete control of its technology ecosystem. You can read my most recent report here:

Tesla Will Need To Prove The Viability Of Its Robotaxi Program In June.

Looking Under The Hood Of TSLL

TSLL is a leveraged ETF, meaning that the portfolio managers actively manage a derivatives strategy to achieve the mandated goal: producing 2x the daily return on the underlying stock, TSLA. The ETF was launched on August 9, 2022, and holds an expense ratio of 96bps. TSLL has an average daily volume of 98mm shares trading hands, making this ETF a highly liquid asset for active trading.

TSLL holds various TSLA Swaps, TSLA shares, and an assortment of Treasuries that are likely held as collateral against the swap positions.

Naturally, TSLL will hold certain inherent risks as it relates to the strategy. Similar to holding your entire portfolio in a single stock, TSLL has complete exposure to TSLA shares and entirely depends on the performance of the underlying stock.

The portfolio also holds a substantial amount of leverage, meaning that the sum of the total holdings is larger than the net asset value. In this case, the total holdings of the ETF strategy amount to 309.90%. This component is necessary in order for the strategy to reach its goal, to achieve 2x the daily return of TSLA shares.

Comparing TSLA shares to TSLL, it is clear that the strategy performs very close to its intended goal.

On the other end of the spectrum, TSLL shares will result in 2x the daily loss of the underlying stock. This means if TSLA shares decline by 1%, TSLL shares will decline by 2% within a single day.

This brings forth the risk of default in the instance of extreme volatility. Though not very likely, if TSLA shares were to experience a -50% decline in a single day, TSLL shares would drop to 0. Again, though this isn’t a likely scenario, it must be considered as the worst-case-scenario that can occur in times of market stress.

Do bear in mind that this pricing chart is framed within a single day. As part of the strategy, the basis swaps reset daily, meaning that returns, or losses, can compound when TSLL is held beyond a single day. This means that multiple days of sequential gains or multiple days of sequential losses can effectively amplify gains or losses when held.

If an investor were to have held TSLL shares since inception, the investor would have experienced a loss of -19.82% rather than a gain of 25.59% when holding TSLA shares. It is clear that extending one’s holding period has the potential of compounding losses beyond realized returns. Granted, if one were to hold this strategy during an upcycle, the return profile will look entirely different.

The point that I’m trying to draw across is that due to the daily reset of the derivatives strategy, TSLL will not achieve a 2x return beyond the daily holding period. This also means that if TSLA shares were to decline by -50% over a 30-day period rather than a single day, TSLL will not reach 0 because of the daily reset. This contrasts with a single-day decline of -50% as suggested above. One interesting factor is that TSLL pays out a quarterly distribution as well as an annual short-term capital gain. Though this is a benefit to shareholders, I do not believe this feature should materially impact an investor’s decision whether to trade TSLL given that TSLL is meant to be actively traded on a daily basis and not held over time.

Please review the risks of leveraged ETFs as explained in the linked SEC bulletin before placing any trades in NVDL. These risks include extreme volatility, time and volatility decay, and the real possibility of losing all of your investment. The normal risks can be exaggerated with single-stock ETFs such as TSLL. Only experienced traders who fully understand the risks should trade them. This article further explains the important risks of such ETFs with examples.

Conclusion

TSLL is a leveraged ETF strategy that seeks to achieve 2x TSLA shares’ daily performance. This portfolio is designed to be actively traded and held for no longer than a single day. Beyond this, TSLL’s performance may be amplified beyond the 2x target, whether to the upside or downside. Given my bullish position on TSLA, I believe TSLL can provide traders a good vehicle to actively manage a position in Tesla.

data.title: Healthy Air Traffic And Improving Aftermarket Outlook Haven't Helped Melrose All That Much
data.description:
Despite a pretty constructive environment, shares of Melrose (OTCPK:MLSPF) (MRO.L) haven’t performed especially well since my last update. Up about 5%, Melrose has done okay relative to some other suppliers like Hexcel (HXL), Moog (MOG-A) (MOG-B), Thales (OTCPK:THLLY), and Woodward (WWD), but engine suppliers better leveraged to improving outlooks for deliveries from the major aircraft companies have shot ahead, with MTU Aero Engines (OTCPK:MTUAF), Rolls Royce (OTCPK:RYCEY), GE (GE), and Safran (OTCPK:SAFRY) have shot ahead with those stocks up 20% to 50% over the same time.

While longer-term comparisons are more favorable to Melrose, it’s nevertheless apparent that this isn’t a hot ticket at the moment, and I do have some concerns that investor attention will be more focused on names with more original equipment exposure in 2025 as Airbus (OTCPK:EADSY) and Boeing (BA) look to be in better shape with respect to hitting production targets. I do think that Melrose remains undervalued enough to offer a worthwhile return, but this is a name that would definitely benefit from a strong beat-and-raise report when management reports full 2024 results in early March.

The Aftermarket Story Is Still Positive

Although Melrose generates the majority of its reported revenue (by a large margin) from original equipment, the profitability of the aftermarket business is considerably better and the aftermarket business is likely to grow from around 40% of profits in 2023 to around two-thirds of profits by the end of this decade. Suffice it to say, then, that this is an important part of the business.

Despite wobbly macroeconomic indicators around the world (including consumer confidence), air traffic has remained pretty healthy. IATA hasn’t yet published numbers for January air traffic, but December was up almost 9%, with full-year traffic up 10% against a roughly 4% increase in flight cycles. With flight cycles up about 5% in January, I’d expect another high single-digit to low double-digit number for traffic, and the fourth quarter reporting cycle was a strong one for U.S. airlines with multiple beat-and-raise quarters. Moreover, with global air fares tracking up 9% to 12% yoy in January, I’d say it’s not just a U.S. phenomenon.

This is good news for Melrose, as normal wear-and-tear on engines in service will eventually drive engine shop visits for the engines covered by the company’s Risk and Revenue Sharing Partnerships (or RRSPs) that account for almost half of the company’s profitability, as well as its growing repair business.

Better still, the combination of growing air traffic demand and production challenges at Airbus and Boeing are leading to lower than expected aircraft retirements, and those older aircraft in service require more aftermarket service/repair activity to keep flying. With the company actively expanding its repair business (and adding products to its repair portfolio like LEAP-1A and LEAP-1B fan blades) and present in the U.S., Europe, and Asia, this business should be able to grow from around 5% of total revenue today to closer to 10% by 2030 and with strong margins.

A Smoother Trajectory For OEM Is Positive

Whie I expect the aftermarket business to be the main driver of profit and free cash flow growth for Melrose, the original equipment business still matters – if for no other reason than it feeds future aftermarket demand and growth.

Looking back at Melrose’s third quarter update, it was clear that OE activity remains challenged, as overall Engine revenue grew 17%, but aftermarket grew 32%, while Structures revenue (which is largely OE) grew 1%. Issues with other Airbus/Boeing suppliers, particularly around areas like casting and forging, continued to be problematic, but recent reports from Airbus and Boeing (including good January deliveries) support some optimism that production is on a better, more predictable path.

With almost a decade of narrowbody and widebody business in the books for both Airbus and Boeing, I expect good growth across the next decade for Melrose. This won’t be especially profitable business upfront, but eventually these engines and aircraft will require routine maintenance and repairs, and that’s when the margin leverage really comes into play.

Visibility Remains A Challenge On Multiple Fronts

There are issues with the Melrose model that investors need to be aware of and consider.

Arguably the biggest is that the structure of the company’s RRSP leads to complicated and somewhat opaque accounting, as well as mismatches between revenue and cash flow recognition. Management has tried to be more transparent (including the offering of this “booklet” in late October), and the mismatches should shrink as engine shop visits increase over time (making the timing of FCF generation more predictable), but this has been an issue for some time and some investors don’t want to bother with companies with accounting “complications”.

There are also ongoing challenges with the profitability of the Structures business. Management has been doing the right things to improve structural profitability (consolidating sites, repricing or exiting unprofitable business, et al), but the Structures business will never be as profitable as the Engines business.

Last and not least, there are inherently unpredictable macro drivers to this business. While another global pandemic doesn’t seem particularly likely, the reality is that air travel demand is economically-sensitive and there is considerably more economic uncertainty now than a year ago. Not all of this uncertainty is necessarily bad for Melrose though – with less certainty about international security agreements, European countries could start spending more on their militaries, which would ultimately be a modest positive for Melrose.

The Outlook

Melrose seems on track for a 2024 that was basically as I expected; I’ve trimmed my revenue expectations slightly on weaker OE business, but by and large the year has developed as I expected. I likewise haven’t made major changes to my longer-term estimates, as drivers like lower aircraft retirements and increased aftermarket spending on those aircraft have been part of my thesis and model for a while.

I’m looking for around 9% revenue growth over the next five years and longer-term growth above 7% as the company benefits from expanding aftermarket demand and growing aircraft deliveries. I expect around 200-250bp of EBITDA margin improvement over the next three to five years, and I believe the maturation of the aftermarket business (and growth in the repair business) can help drive free cash flow margins toward the mid-to-high teens, driving significant free cash flow growth over the next decade.

Management has been pretty consistent about not being interested in major M&A, and so I would expect those future cash flows will eventually fund larger dividends and buybacks. I could, perhaps, see the company acquiring assets in the repair space to accelerate the growth of that business (more locations/customers or adding capabilities it doesn’t currently have), but I would be surprised if the company splashed out to add further exposure to engine components.

Between discounted cash flow and EV/EBITDA (using a 12.5x multiple, down from my prior 13.5x to reflect further maturation of the cycle), I believe Melrose shares are around 10% to 15% undervalued today.

The Bottom Line

I’m not sure 10% to 15% undervaluation is enough to make a forceful “buy” argument, particularly if there’s further rotation from aftermarket-driven names toward original equipment names. As is, I don’t dislike this company or stock, but it seems like a name that needs a meaningfully positive guidance update from management at the upcoming earnings release to renew investor interest and enthusiasm.
